planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm soil or transplant seedlings; Korean perilla (kkaennip) is distinct from Japanese shiso in having larger, thicker, rounder, more strongly aromatic leaves with a more complex anise-mint-basil fragrance used extensively in Korean cuisine as a ssam wrap leaf, banchan pickle, and seasoning — one of the most important herbs in Korean cooking.

directions: Sow seeds on the soil surface (they need light to germinate) or barely cover; Korean perilla self-seeds prolifically and will return each year once established — deadhead if you want to control spread. Perilla frutescens var. japonica (Korean perilla, kkaennip, also called Korean sesame leaf or Korean mint) is distinct from the Japanese shiso (var. crispa) in its larger size, rounder leaf shape, flat rather than crinkled leaf margin, thicker leaf texture, and stronger, more complex fragrance — a blend of anise, mint, basil, and sesame that is deeply characteristic of Korean cuisine. The large (3–5 inch), round-oval, bright green leaves (occasionally flushed with purple on the underside) are used in Korean cooking in numerous ways: as ssam wraps for grilled meats (samgyeopsal, bulgogi), as perilla kimchi (kkaennip kimchi), marinated in soy and sesame as a banchan side dish, fried in batter as pajeon ingredients, and as a culinary herb in soups and stews. Distinguished from Japanese shiso by larger leaf size and the more potent, less floral fragrance. Excellent for hot, humid Gulf Coast gardens where it thrives in summer heat; tolerates partial shade.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension Asian Vegetables Guide

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm soil or start indoors 4–6 weeks before transplanting; feather celosia is a distinct form of celosia from the cockscomb (cristata) type, producing upright, feathery, silky plume-like flower heads in brilliant shades of red, orange, yellow, pink, and purple that are among the most striking warm-season bedding and cut flowers for Gulf Coast and Southern gardens.

directions: Sow seeds on the soil surface after last frost (they need some light for germination); thin to 6–12 inches depending on variety size. Celosia argentea var. plumosa (feather or plume celosia) is one of the three main celosia types — the others being the brain/cockscomb cristata form and the wheat-spike spicata form — distinguished by its upright, tapering, silky, feathery plume-like flower heads that resemble ostrich feathers or soft velvet flames in brilliant, saturated colors. The plumosa types are among the most brilliantly colored warm-season annuals available for bedding, cut flower, and container gardens; flowers are extraordinarily heat-tolerant, blooming continuously through Gulf Coast and Southern summers that defeat most other annuals. Colors range from deepest crimson red through orange, gold, peach, pink, and creamy white. Fresh flowers have a unique velvety texture; dried flowers hold their color well for wreaths and dried arrangements. The foliage and flowers of all celosia types are edible (used as vegetables in parts of Africa where Celosia argentea is an important leaf vegetable) and highly nutritious.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; Green Ischia is one of the oldest continuously grown fig varieties, documented in cultivation since at least the 16th century, producing small to medium, greenish-yellow fruits with sweet amber flesh that are exceptional for both fresh eating and drying.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile to average soil in full sun; Green Ischia is a common fig type (no pollinator required) and typically produces both a reliable breba crop and a main crop. Green Ischia (also called Ischia Verde or simply 'Ischia') is one of the oldest continuously documented fig cultivars in European cultivation, with recorded references going back to at least the 16th century — one of the ancient Italian figs associated with the island of Ischia in the Bay of Naples. The small to medium, round to pear-shaped fruits have greenish-yellow skin that turns to golden-yellow when fully ripe; the interior flesh is pale amber to strawberry-amber with excellent sweetness and mild, delicate fig flavor with a gentle honey note. A two-crop variety that typically produces a moderate breba crop in early summer and a more abundant main crop in August–September. The small tree size (usually 8–15 feet with mature girth) makes Green Ischia more manageable than large fig varieties and an excellent choice for container culture in zones north of 7 where frost protection is needed. Outstanding fresh and dried.

planting.note: Direct sow in hills after last frost in rich, well-drained soil; Banana squash is one of the largest commonly grown winter squash varieties, producing enormously long (18–36+ inches), cylindrical, banana-shaped fruits in pale pink-orange-salmon skin with dense, sweet, golden-orange flesh — a Western US market and home-garden staple sold in cut wedges because the fruits are too large for whole-fruit sale.

directions: Sow 1 inch deep in hills or rows; Banana squash requires a long season (105–120 days) and is best suited to zones 5–11 with warm summers; in short-season areas, start indoors 3–4 weeks before transplanting. Banana squash is a Cucurbita maxima type producing some of the longest and heaviest fruits of any winter squash, with cylindrical, slightly curved, banana-shaped fruits typically 18–36 inches long and weighing 10–35 lbs; the distinctive pale pink-salmon to peachy-orange skin makes it instantly recognizable and it is commonly sold in cut wedges at Western US grocery stores and farmers' markets where the whole fruit would be too large for most households. The flesh is dense, smooth-textured, golden-orange with excellent sweetness and a rich, slightly nutty flavor excellent for roasting, soups, and pies. A vigorous, sprawling vine requiring considerable space (at least 8 feet in each direction); store harvested fruits in a cool, dry location where they keep for 3–6 months.

planting.note: Direct sow in hills after last frost; Turban squash is both an ornamental and edible winter squash producing the most architecturally dramatic fruits in the cucurbit family — a protruding 'cap' or acorn-like secondary lobe emerges from the top of the main squash creating the distinctive turban shape in brilliant orange and cream and green swirling patterns.

directions: Sow 1 inch deep after last frost in rich, well-amended soil in full sun; provide 6–8 feet of space for sprawling vines. Turban squash (also called Turk's Cap, Turk's Turban, or French Turban) is one of the most visually distinctive of all winter squash, producing medium to large (5–10 lb) fruits with a dramatic secondary lobe or 'cap' protruding from the blossom end in a shape resembling a Turkish turban or Ottoman fez — this unusual morphology is a Cucurbita maxima trait that occasionally appears in several varieties but is most pronounced and reliable in this dedicated 'Turban' selection. The skin is spectacularly patterned in orange, cream, and green with mottled, swirling patterns that vary between fruits; the ornamental quality makes Turban squash one of the most popular fall decorative squashes alongside regular pumpkins. The flesh is edible with a mild, somewhat dry, floury texture and pleasant squash flavor well-suited to stuffing — the cap portion is often removed and used as a natural serving bowl for soups or stuffings.
